words: IONE ADAIR:  "Uncle Sam" Owings, used to run the Times Democrat here in Moscow
years and years ago, and he loved fishing, and he wanted to go fishing
with us, and we went fishing. So my sister and I decided we'd break
down once and take him with us. We took him up to Elk River and he
caught sight of the bridge, the old bridge over the river, and he sat
down with his pole by that bridge and fished like you would for cat
fish in Carolina- that's where he was from- and he sat there all afternoon
and fished that bridge and Marjie and I fished up and
down the stream and we had a nice basketful of fish between us when
we got through. Well,coming in Uncle Sam decided he'd sing for us.
And we'd rather have him sing than smoke those old cigars that he smo
ked. So, we told him to sing for us. We asked him if he knew any
songs, "Oh, yes," he said, "I know a song." So, he started in before
we left Bovill to sing, and he sang verse after verse after verse til
we landed down here and put him on the lot where the employment agency
is now. That was their home. And we let him out there with an empty
basket and a song from Bovill to Moscow.
BERNADINE ADAIR CORNELISON:  What was the song? You told me that once.

speaker: IA
words: I told you once, but I can't remember the name of the song. Verse af

speaker: IA
words: ter verse, after verse. Verse after verse, I think he must have made

speaker: IA
words: it up as he went along! It couldn't have been a song that long!

speaker: IA
words: SAM SCHRAGER:  He didn't catch any fish?

speaker: IA
words: No, just that old Southern way.

speaker: SS
words: He didn't catch any fish on the bridge?

speaker: IA
words: No, he didn't catch any fish.

speaker: SS
words: You'd think he'd learn from you how to do it. He'd want to go up and

speaker: SS
words: down the stream.

speaker: BC
words: He didn't like to move around much.

speaker: IA
words: He had fished in Carolina and he knew how to fish in Carolina; they

speaker: IA
words: were catfish, I think. He knew how to fish there, but he didn't know

speaker: IA
words: anything about going up and down the stream and watching the holes and

speaker: IA
words: watching for the fish and catching them when they got on. Didn't know

speaker: IA
words: a thing about that. He just waited for the fish to come and take his

speaker: IA
words: bait, and if they got on and got caught, why, he had a fish.

speaker: SS

speaker: IA

speaker: IA
words: neath somewhere.

speaker: SS
words: No I don't think-

speaker: BC

speaker: IA

speaker: IA
words: You could have any size you wanted; any size you could catch. And

speaker: IA
words: there was no limit to fishing.

speaker: SS
words: Was it your father that taught you how to fish? Out there?

speaker: IA
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: You mentioned to me that you went on the streams with him.

speaker: IA
words: I always went with him.

speaker: BC
words: Wading.

speaker: IA
words: When he wanted to go fishing, I'd go with him whether any of the rest

speaker: IA
words: of them would go or not. I always went with my father fishing. And

speaker: IA
words: we always caught all of our fish that we needed. And Daddy and Dr.

speaker: IA
words: Hatfield fished for years up and down that old Elk River stream. They

speaker: IA
words: knew every hole and I think they knew all the trout by name! And

speaker: IA
words: if we caught Johnny today, we'd catch James tomorrow! (Chuckles)

speaker: IA
words: But, we'd drive up, we'd leave early in the morning so as to get up

speaker: IA
words: there just about daybreak, about the time the sun was up and we'd

speaker: IA
words: start fishing. And we were always through early. And we had great

speaker: IA
words: times fishing up and down there. My father had waders , and they

speaker: IA
words: came onto your feet, rubber, and came up above his waistline and then

speaker: IA
words: he got a And he'd take one hole and take anotner or

speaker: IA
words: if it was a big one, we'd both take the hole, but a distrubance in two

speaker: IA
words: places would cause the fish to bite a littlelower, a little farther

speaker: IA
words: down. And when we were fishing with angle worms, we did it that way,

speaker: IA
words: and when fishing with grasshoppers, we had to get out and spank our

speaker: IA
words: grasshoppers while it was damp in the morning before they began to

speaker: IA
words: get lively. They are always very stupid at night, and as soon as it

speaker: IA
words: gets cold, the grasshopper'11 fold up his wings and stay right down

speaker: IA
words: next to the ground where its warmer, and they'd spring as the warm

speaker: IA
words: air comes then they get up and climb up on the grass, and you take

speaker: IA
words: a flat piece of shingle or a flyswatter, if you had one, they're the

words: things to catch them with, and we'd swat those grasshoppers and put

speaker: IA
words: 'em in a bait can and then swat the next one and put him in the bait

speaker: IA
words: can.

speaker: BC
words: Daddy used to raise worms in amulch down here; he raised his fishing

speaker: BC
words: worms.

speaker: IA
words: Under those old willow trees he put manure and leaves and then he

words: would put coffee grounds and lettuce- wilted lettuce leaves, on top

speaker: IA
words: of that and then keep it covered so that nothing could get in and

speaker: IA
words: catch his worms. He had a kind of screen that went over the top of

speaker: IA
words: the tub and he kept all of his worms in the tub for going fishing. And

speaker: IA
words: when he went in the spring before the grasshoppers got lively, we'd

speaker: IA
words: fish with angleworms. And late in the fall you fished again with

speaker: IA
words: angleworms. And during the summer you fished with grasshoppers.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of fishing gear did you use?

speaker: IA

words: got one, because he thought that I was catching as many fish as he

words: did. Maybe his pole wasn't right. And so he got himself a collap

words: when you pull 'em out you have a long sized pole.

speaker: SS
words: Easy for carrying?

speaker: IA
words: Yes, easy for carrying, when you're in the car or when you're going

speaker: IA
words: to the fishing grounds.

speaker: SS

speaker: IA

speaker: IA
words: Just anything to wind your line up on and keep it from getting tangled.

speaker: IA
words: And Dddy had a long one; his had a ratchet that would wind it up and

speaker: IA
words: a handle on it that you could wind it up. And his was a real spinning. And mine was

speaker: IA
words: just an ordinary ten cent reel. But I caught just

speaker: IA
words: as many fish as Dad did. (Chuckles)

speaker: BC
words: Caught more, lots of times.

speaker: SS
words: Were the trout big then?

speaker: IA
words: They were much larger than they were later, because the later ones

speaker: IA
words: were younger, and the ones that hadn't been caught the year before

speaker: IA
words: would manage to grow up. We were the first people that fished in Elk

speaker: IA
words: River.

words: And that's another tale. You told him that once.

speaker: IA

words: and we went over to the Fourth of July celebration and fished for the

words: and put them in Elk River and that was above the Elk River Falls, and

words: home and had a shipment of Eastern brook trout, and they put them in

words: down by the bridge at Elk River there.

speaker: IA
words: He wouldn't let anyone fish.

speaker: BC
words: Started something.

speaker: IA
words: But, Mrs. Mc Bride asked my two sisters, Marjorie and Lulu and I out

words: to spend the summer with her at her cabin on and

speaker: IA
words: while we were there Mr. Trumble sent word over that they were having

words: over. And we went over to the homestead, and while wei were there he

words: in Elk River.

speaker: BC
words: Tell him about the first dandelions. Have you ever heard that story?

speaker: SS
words: No.

speaker: IA
words: Oh, that was in My father came West in the fall of

speaker: IA
words: '93 and that was the fall when we had so much rain that the farmers

speaker: IA
words: couldn't get in to get their wheat. If it was cut it was down and it

speaker: IA
words: couldn't be saved, and if it was sacked it was in the fields and they

speaker: IA
words: couldn't even get in to salvage the sacked grain. It sprouted right

speaker: IA
words: in the sacks. They couldn't get it. And, this old gentleman who had

speaker: IA
words: been in Kansas from quite near where my father had been practicing,

speaker: IA
words: found out that Daddy was from Kansas and he came up to make his ac

speaker: IA
words: quaintance. And than he invited Dad over to his place, and he said,

speaker: IA
words: "Doctor, do you like dandelion greens?" And Daddy said, "I don't know,

speaker: IA
words: I've never eaten dandelion greens. I've eaten beet greens and mustard

speaker: IA
words: greens, but never dandelion." "Well," he said,"you send the girls over

speaker: IA
words: and I'll give you a mess of dandelion greens." And Daddy said, "Where

speaker: IA
words: did you get dandelion greens?" He said, "I sent back to Kansas and got

speaker: IA
words: the seeds the dandelions. I'm raising dandelions for my greens."

speaker: IA
words: And that, I think, was the beginning of the dandelions in this part of

speaker: IA
words: the country. But that's how it happened. They lived over on Van

speaker: IA
words: Buren Street, just the other side where the telephone building is on

speaker: IA
words: Van Buren- isn't it? And on the left-hand of the street, beyond the

speaker: IA
words: telephone, on the left-hand side of the street, there was a house that

words: stood up on a hill that had three separate buildings; there was a

speaker: IA
words: basement and the upper story and a story on top of that, and that's

words: where this old gentleman from Kansas lived.

speaker: BC
words: Didn't you tell me his name was Andrews or Anderson?

speaker: IA
words: Andrews or Anderson, I'm not sure which.

speaker: BC
words: He started something.

speaker: IA
words: At any rate-

speaker: SS

words: after the harvest to get through the winter?

words: Building and there was a stairway that went down to the alley and we

words: and forth every once in a while to see how Mother and the children

words: money enough to pay him. And when my father first came to the Creightons

speaker: IA
words: your sister, at Johnson." And Dad said, "Why, yes, that's what

speaker: IA
words: I said." And he said, "Well, there is a gentleman over at the store

words: and went to pay the bill, he said charge that to Doctor Adair." And

words: Mr. Hooper said, "I just asked him to wait until I check with you,

words: for I understood you to say that you only had the one sister with two

words: children." And he said, "Yes." So Mr. Hooper said, "What'11 I do with

speaker: IA
words: the gentleman?" Dad said, "Just tell him, nothing doing!" He said,

words: "I am a stranger in this part of the country and have a family of my

speaker: IA
words: own," and he says, "I'm not outfitting anyone's children but my own.

speaker: IA
words: And I'll do well to do that." So the children didn't get it. It was

speaker: IA
words: OP a family from Potlatch or Princeton here somewhere.

words: up at Princeton.

speaker: IA

words: first time he'd been in town and he thought he saw a chance of out

words: fitting the children for school that fall.

words: the area, there's a certain class of people that go and take out

words: couldn't get anything.
